name: Ma Diao (馬吊) — Chinese Money-Suited Cards (the Deep Root)
description:
# Ma Diao (馬吊) — Chinese money-suited cards

## At a glance

The **deepest root of the playing-card form itself** — and, deliberately, *not* a direct ancestor of tarot. Paper playing cards are usually traced to **Tang-dynasty China** (618–907 CE) — though the famous 9th-c. 'leaf game' reference is disputed (it may describe a book or board game), and the earliest *unambiguous* paper playing cards are Yuan-era, c. 1294; by the time of the Song and Ming they had developed into **money-suited** packs whose suits are denominations of currency. **Ma Diao** (馬吊, 'hanging horse') is the best-documented of these games, codified in the **late Ming** (16th–17th c.) and described in period manuals. This library includes it to show where the *idea* of a suited pack of cards begins, several removes upstream of anything European.

## The money suits

Four ascending money denominations: **cash → strings of cash → myriads → tens-of-myriads** (文 · 索 · 萬 · 十萬). The suits are not abstract pips but units of money — the structural signature of the East-Asian tradition. The **myriad (萬) suit** was, in many sets, illustrated with the 108 bandit-heroes of the novel *Water Margin* (水滸傳), so painted 'Water Margin cards' (水滸牌) are a famous money-suited variety still made in folk workshops today.

## Relationship to tarot — a deep root, not a parent

The standard scholarly thesis (Wilkinson, Needham, Lo) is that the playing-card *form* diffused **westward out of China**, reaching Europe through the **Islamic world**, where the **Mamluk pack** (Egypt/Syria) is the deck Europe directly copied. So the line to tarot runs **China → (centuries, many intermediaries) → Mamluk → Italian/Spanish suits → trionfi/tarot**. Ma Diao is a **cousin and a deep root**, like Ganjifa — it shows the origin of the form, but no card here descends by a clean line into a tarot trump. The detail of the transmission is genuinely debated; what is not debated is that paper cards are a Chinese invention.

## Game, never divination

Ma Diao is a **trick-and-draw gambling game** for four players, played with a 40-card money-suited pack. There is no cartomantic tradition attached to it — it sits in this library purely as the upstream of the *object*, not of any reading practice.

## Provenance & evidence

The cards shown are the **public-domain holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden** (high-resolution museum scans on Wikimedia Commons), a European collection of an East-Asian pack. Twelve items survive there. Individual suit/value identification is left open rather than guessed.

## Sources

- W. H. Wilkinson, 'Chinese Origin of Playing Cards' (1895); Joseph Needham, *Science and Civilisation in China*, vol. 5; Andrew Lo on Ma Diao and Water Margin cards; Dummett, *The Game of Tarot* (1980), and Dummett & McLeod, *A History of Games Played with the Tarot Pack* (2004) (on the China→Islam→Europe diffusion). Card images: Skokloster Castle / Wikimedia Commons, public domain.

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13).** The money-suited form is firmly attested from Lu Rong (1436–94, late 15th c.); Ma Diao itself is codified in the late Ming (16th–17th c.). The 'c. 1600' date and the 17th–18th-c. European (Skokloster) collection reference are loosely sourced — treat the dating as approximate. See [research/decks/madiao-money-cards.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/decks/madiao-money-cards.md).
_source: Wikimedia Commons (Skokloster Castle), public domain. Hotlinked with attribution.

sections.What it is: The lowest money suit: single copper coins (wén). The coin is the ancestor of the European 'Coins/Pentacles' suit — the one suit that survived the long diffusion west more or less recognisably.
sections.Scene: Cards of the 文錢 (wénqián) cash suit, representing individual copper coins with a square hole at the centre. Pip cards show the coin symbol — a round disc with square opening — in numerical arrangements. No surviving image in this grammar.
sections.Symbol: The cash coin was the basic monetary unit of Ming-dynasty China. In the card suits it corresponds to the single coin, the irreducible monetary atom.

sections.What it is: Strings or ropes (suǒ) on which coins were threaded — each string a bundle of one hundred cash. A denomination, not an abstract pip: the suits of this pack are literally units of money.
sections.Research note:
Ropes or strings on which coins were threaded — each string a bundle of (about) a hundred cash. A denomination, not an abstract pip (9 cards, 1–9) [@web_madiao_wiki]. 

*Sources:* [@web_madiao_wiki], [@web_suit_wiki].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/madiao-money-cards.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/madiao-money-cards.md).
sections.Scene: Cards of the 索子 (suǒzǐ) strings-of-cash suit, representing multiple coins strung together on a cord. Pip cards show string or bundle symbols. No surviving image in this grammar.
sections.Symbol: Strung coins — the standard way of transporting and counting large amounts of cash in Ming China — gave the suit its name and symbol.

sections.What it is: Ten-thousand-cash units (wàn). In many money-card sets this suit was illustrated with the 108 bandit-heroes of the novel *Water Margin* (水滸傳) — so 'Water Margin cards' (水滸牌) are a famous money-suited variety.
sections.Scene: Cards of the 萬貫 (wànguàn) myriads suit, representing ten thousand strings of cash — an abstract denomination of extraordinary wealth. No surviving image in this grammar.
sections.Symbol: The myriads suit encodes large-scale wealth, moving the deck into the realm of abstract monetary value beyond physical cash.

sections.What it is: The top suit (the 'decade' / tens-of-myriads), carrying the honour cards. The four ascending money denominations — cash → strings → myriads → tens-of-myriads — are the whole structure of the game.
sections.Research note:
The highest suit, carrying the honour cards; in the Madiao pack it runs from 20-myriad up through hundred-, thousand-, and myriad-myriad (11 cards) [@web_madiao_wiki]. 

*Sources:* [@web00c_islamic_chinese], [@web_madiao_wiki].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/madiao-money-cards.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/madiao-money-cards.md).
sections.Scene: Cards of the 十萬 (shíwàn) tens-of-myriads suit, representing hundreds of thousands of cash. No surviving image in this grammar.
sections.Symbol: The fourth suit operates at the highest abstract monetary denomination, completing the progression from single coin to inconceivable wealth.

sections.What it is: Ma Diao is a *money-suited* pack: its four suits are ascending denominations of cash, not the cups/swords/coins/batons of the Mamluk and European line. This is the structural signature of the East-Asian card tradition.
sections.Scene: The four suits of the Ma Diao pack together encode a monetary ladder: cash (individual coins), strings (coin bundles), myriads (ten thousand strings), and tens of myriads. No composite image survives; this is a conceptual grouping.
sections.Symbol: The four-suit money structure of Ma Diao is proposed as one pathway through which suit-card structure (later mapped onto cups, coins, swords, and batons in Europe) may have disseminated westward via trade routes.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: A woodblock-printed card on thick paper. The upper third contains a panel with bold Chinese characters. The centre shows a stylised radiating sun-like or face-like form rendered in black ink, surrounded by radiating lines. The lower third has angular geometric patterns — repeated arrowhead and diagonal shapes — in a grid.
sections.Symbol: This card belongs to the money suits (likely a high pip or court card). The radiating sun-form in the centre and the angular lower border are characteristic of the expressive woodblock printing style of 17th century Chinese playing cards.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: A woodblock card with a Chinese character panel at the upper left (including the character 五 — five). The centre shows a crescent or curved shape with hatched internal geometry and angular surrounding decoration. The lower portion has dotted and triangular border patterns.
sections.Symbol: The character 五 (five) identifies this as a five-pip card, likely in the strings suit. The curved central motif may represent a string bundle or a suit symbol in abstracted form.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: A woodblock-printed card showing a human figure — a court official or deity — rendered in frontal bust view. The face is detailed with clear features; the head bears a distinctive cap or hat. The lower body is shown in linear abstract style, surrounded by geometric hatching.
sections.Symbol: This is a court card in the Ma Diao system, depicting a historical or mythological figure. The Ming-era money card courts often depicted famous generals, officials, or characters from popular literature.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: A woodblock card with a Chinese character panel at top (including 石有 or similar). Below, a court figure is shown in a three-quarter or frontal view, wearing a distinctive official hat. Chinese characters appear on the body of the figure, identifying them by name or title.
sections.Symbol: A named official card: the text on the body of court figures in Ma Diao identifies them specifically, connecting the game to historical memory and vernacular storytelling traditions.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: A woodblock pip card with three geometric motifs arranged vertically — each a nested diamond or square set within concentric lines, suggesting a stylised coin or token symbol. The card has no character panel; it is a pure pip.
sections.Symbol: Three abstract coin-symbols: a pip card in the cash or strings suit, its value communicated entirely through geometric repetition.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: A woodblock pip card showing six round coin symbols arranged in a 2×3 grid. Each coin shows concentric circles with a square or geometric centre — the standard Chinese cash coin motif. The coins are clearly differentiated by their interior patterns.
sections.Symbol: Six cash coins: a 六 (six) pip card in the cash suit. The coin motif preserves the circular form of actual metal currency, making the card a direct visual analogue of money.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: An abstract woodblock card with two distinct zones: the upper half carries a dense angular star or cross pattern of intersecting diagonal lines; the lower half shows concentric arc or rainbow-shaped lines. There is no figure or character panel.
sections.Symbol: A pip or decorative element card whose meaning depends on the game's internal rules. The two-zone composition — angular above, concentric below — may encode a specific suit combination or card type.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: A woodblock pip card showing six oval or rounded coin symbols arranged in a 2×3 grid. The coins are rendered in slightly rounder, more fluid form than the square-centred cash coins on card 102356, suggesting a different suit or denomination.
sections.Symbol: Six coins of a different suit denomination — the oval form may represent 'strings' of cash rather than individual coins, giving this a 六索 (six of strings) reading.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: A woodblock card showing a single large abstract composition: the upper half features a concentric square or rectangular pattern (nested squares converging to a centre), while the lower half is filled with cross-hatch geometric lines. The design fills the entire card field.
sections.Symbol: This card's geometric abstraction suggests a special function card — possibly a wild card, a high-value trump, or a boundary marker within the Ma Diao game structure.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: A woodblock card showing a stylised animal or creature figure rendered in bold linear strokes. The form suggests a dragon or mythological beast — sinuous body, legs visible at bottom, energetic movement conveyed through diagonal brushwork.
sections.Symbol: Figurative court cards in Ma Diao sometimes depict mythological creatures alongside historical officials. The dragon is the supreme symbol of imperial power in Chinese culture.

sections.About: One of the surviving Ma Diao cards in the Chinese holdings at Skokloster Castle, Sweden — a 17th–18th-century European collection of an East-Asian money-suited pack. The individual suit and value are not separately catalogued here; the card is shown as a physical survivor of the tradition, not assigned a reading.
sections.Scene: A damaged card — the upper-left corner is torn away. Two rounded coin symbols are visible at the top register and a third in the centre, above horizontal stripe patterns in the lower half. The design is consistent with a low-value pip card.
sections.Symbol: Even in its damaged state the card preserves the coin motif. The damage itself records the cards' age and use: they were played with hands until they wore.

sections.About: Several Ma Diao cards together (catalogued as 'Mo Diao') from the same Skokloster holdings.
sections.Scene: A museum photograph showing all eleven surviving Skokloster Ma Diao cards spread on a surface, accompanied by a folded paper wrapper inscribed in Swedish 'Kinesiska kort' (Chinese cards). The variety of designs is visible: coin-pip grids, court figures with character labels, geometric abstracts, and decorative cards in one ensemble.
sections.Symbol: The Swedish inscription confirms the cards arrived at Skokloster Castle (Sweden) as curiosities, probably via Dutch East India Company trade networks. Their preservation in a European collection — rather than being played to destruction in China — explains why they survive.

sections.What it is: The twelve Ma Diao items that survive in Sweden at Skokloster Castle — a fragmentary but high-resolution, openly public-domain witness to a money-suited Chinese pack.
sections.Scene: The complete Skokloster Ma Diao set: eleven individual cards (accession numbers 102351–102361) plus a separate sheet (13617), all held at Skokloster Castle, Sweden. The cards are woodblock-printed on heavy paper, showing a mix of pip cards (coin grids, geometric abstracts) and court cards (figural representations of officials or deities).
sections.Symbol: The Skokloster set is among the oldest surviving East Asian playing cards held in a European institution, and the most important physical evidence for the Ma Diao money-card tradition. The set demonstrates the full iconographic range of the genre: abstract pip cards, figured courts, and possibly special-function cards.
